engineCodes.h2: Skoda Engine Codes & Average Rebuilt Prices (UK Supply)
engineCodes.h3: Compare costs for every major Skoda engine — EA211, EA288 and EA888 series. All prices are typical UK market ranges for professionally rebuilt engines with warranty. Click "Get Quote" on any engine code to receive tailored prices from our network of UK Skoda specialists.
filters: Legacy/MPI based on the engine groups listed below.

groups.name: EA211 Petrol Family
groups.failureNote: Common failure points (expandable note): EA211 1.0 TSI and 1.2 TSI engines are known for early timing chain stretch and tensioner failure — particularly on 2012–2016 units. Our rebuilt EA211 engines include revised timing chain kits and upgraded tensioners. Carbon buildup on intake valves is also common due to direct injection; rebuilt units are fully decarbonised before assembly.

groups.name: EA288 Diesel Family
groups.failureNote: Common failure points (expandable note): EA288 1.6 and 2.0 TDI engines suffer from DPF regeneration failures (short trips), EGR cooler leaks, and clogged intake manifolds. Our rebuilt EA288 engines include professionally cleaned DPF/EGR systems or replacement units, tested injectors, and new timing belts.

groups.name: EA888 Petrol Family
groups.failureNote: Common failure points (expandable note): EA888 Gen1 and Gen2 engines (1.8/2.0 TFSI) are notorious for timing chain tensioner failure and oil pump drive degradation. Gen3 units (CRTD, CHHA) improved reliability but still suffer from carbon buildup and water pump leaks. Our rebuilt EA888 units include revised chain tensioners, upgraded oil pump drives (where applicable), and fully cleaned intake valves.

groups.name: Legacy MPI & Older TDI
groups.failureNote: Common failure points (expandable note): 1.9 TDI engines (PD unit injector) are robust but suffer from injector loom failure, camshaft wear (on BRM/BLS variants), and turbo actuator issues. MPI petrol engines are generally reliable but age-related wear (head gaskets, piston rings) drives replacements on high-mileage units. Closing note: Not sure which engine code your Skoda uses? Enter your registration number and we'll match you to the exact engine and best quote — instantly. All engines supplied with a minimum 12-month warranty from UK Skoda specialists. commonProblems.h2: Common Skoda Engine Problems — What It Costs to Fix & When Replacement Makes Sense
commonProblems.h3: Skoda’s popularity across UK fleets, family hatchbacks, and tradespeople means high mileages are common — and so is wear on its VW Group engines. The EA211 1.2/1.4 TSI and EA288 1.6/2.0 TDI families dominate replacement requests on EngineMarket, primarily for timing chain wear and diesel emission system failures. Every rebuilt Skoda engine supplied through our network includes upgraded components designed to address the original failure point — plus a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty. Below we break down the four most costly Skoda engine failures, what genuine repair costs look like at a dealer versus a specialist, and when replacement is the only sensible choice.

engineTypes.h2: Skoda Engine Types – Options, Costs & What to Expect
engineTypes.intro: Every Skoda engine type available through our UK network, with typical price ranges and recommendations based on your vehicle's age, condition and your budget.

types.title: Skoda Reconditioned Engines (Recon)
types.description: A stripped, cleaned and fully inspected engine where all worn components are replaced with OEM‑spec parts. Best for owners who want factory reliability without the factory price.
types.priceRange: Typical price range: £1,400 – £3,800 (depending on engine code)

types.title: Skoda Rebuilt Engines
types.description: A complete rebuild with upgraded components addressing known failure points – such as uprated timing chain kits and reinforced oil pump drives – for a longer service life than a standard replacement.

types.title: Used Skoda Engines
types.description: A tested, low‑mileage take‑out unit from a donor vehicle. The most affordable option when budget is the priority, with verified history and warranty available.

types.title: Skoda Remanufactured Engines
types.description: A factory‑spec, as‑new engine: every surface is machined, every component is replaced, and the unit is built to meet exact OEM tolerances. The premium choice for vRS performance models or owners wanting a zero‑mile engine.

types.title: Skoda Refurbished Engines
types.description: A lightly serviced, cleaned, and tested engine that works reliably but hasn't had a complete rebuild. Often a pragmatic, fast solution for older or budget‑sensitive models like the Fabia or Roomster where a full recon isn't economical.

types.title: Skoda Supply & Fit Engines
types.description: Your replacement engine delivered and professionally installed by a UK Skoda specialist. Includes removal of the old unit, fitting, fluids, and a workmanship warranty.
types.priceRange: Typical added cost: £400 – £800 on top of engine price
types.cta: Find Skoda engine supply & fit deals

engineTypes.closing: All engine types include a minimum 12‑month warranty. Prices are typical UK market ranges and depend on exact engine code (EA211, EA288, EA888), supplier, and any additional fitting services. Use our free comparison tool to get a tailored quote for your Skoda model today.

engineSizes.h2: Skoda Engines by Size – Find the Right Displacement for Your Model
engineSizes.intro: Every Skoda engine size explained — with the models and engine codes that used each displacement — so you can quickly identify the correct replacement unit for your vehicle.

items.title: Skoda 1.2L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 1.2L three-cylinder TDI was Skoda's entry-level diesel, offered briefly in the Fabia and Roomster between 2010 and 2014. Based on the EA189 family (code BNM), this unit prioritised economy over power but suffered from DPF clogging on short journeys. Replacement demand remains low, but failure typically forces a full engine swap as parts availability is limited. Engine code(s): BNM Compatible models (UK): Fabia (2007–2014), Roomster (2010–2014) Production years: 2010–2014 Common failure points: DPF blockage, EGR valve sticking injector wear
items.cta: Get quotes for Skoda 1.2L diesel engines

items.title: Skoda 1.4L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 1.4L TDI three-cylinder (EA188 / EA189 family) appeared across Fabia, Rapid, and Roomster models from 2005 to 2015, using engine codes BNV, BMS, and AMF. While economical and torquey for its size, the unit is known for injector seal leaks, turbo actuator failure, and DPF issues in later versions. Many high‑mileage examples now require replacement rather than repair due to parts scarcity and labour costs. Engine code(s): AMF, BNV, BMS, BNM Compatible models (UK): Fabia (2005–2014), Rapid (2012–2015), Roomster (2006–2015) Production years: 2005–2015 Common failure points: Injector seal leakage, turbo actuator failure, DPF clogging (post‑2009)
items.cta: Get quotes for Skoda 1.4L diesel engines

items.title: Skoda 1.6L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 1.6L TDI (EA189 and EA288 families) is Skoda's most common diesel, fitted to Octavia, Superb, Yeti, Karoq, Kodiaq, Fabia, and Rapid between 2009 and 2024. Engine codes include CAYC, CLCA, CRKB, DBKA, and CRVA. The EA189 units suffer from EGR cooler fires, injector wiring faults, and DPF clogging, while EA288 engines add AdBlue injector crystallisation. Replacement demand is extremely high, especially for Octavia fleet cars. Engine code(s): CAYC, CLCA, CRKB, DBKA, CRVA, DGTE Compatible models (UK): Octavia (2009–2020), Superb (2009–2020), Yeti (2009–2017), Karoq (2017–2024), Kodiaq (2017–2024), Fabia (2010–2021), Rapid (2012–2019) Production years: 2009–2024 Common failure points: EA189 – DPF/EGR blockage, injector loom failure; EA288 – AdBlue injector crystallisation, thermostat housing leak
items.cta: Get quotes for Skoda 1.6L diesel engines

items.title: Skoda 2.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.0L TDI (EA189, EA288, EA288 evo) powers higher‑spec Octavia, Superb, Kodiaq, Karoq, and Yeti models from 2004 to present. Engine codes include BKD, BMN, CFHC, CFFB, CKFC, CRLB, DFGA, and DNUA. Known vulnerabilities vary: BKD injector loom failure, BMN DPF cracking, CFHC turbo vane sticking, and EA288 evo oil pump belt failure. This displacement dominates commercial and family fleet replacement searches. Engine code(s): BKD, BMN, AZV, CFHC, CFFB, CLCB, CKFC, CRLB, DFGA, DNUA, DTRB Compatible models (UK): Octavia (2004–2020), Superb (2008–2024), Yeti (2009–2017), Kodiaq (2017–present), Karoq (2017–present), Fabia vRS (2005–2010) Production years: 2004–present Common failure points: Injector loom failure (BKD), DPF cracking (BMN), turbo vane sticking, EA288 evo oil pump belt failure
items.cta: Get quotes for Skoda 2.0L diesel engines

items.title: Skoda 1.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.0L three‑cylinder TSI (EA211 family) arrived in 2015 and now powers Fabia, Rapid, Scala, Kamiq, and Octavia. Engine codes include CHZB, CHZJ, DKLA, DKLC, and DLLA. This award‑winning unit is generally robust, but common replacement triggers include wet belt failure (early versions), injector failure, and high‑mileage turbo wastegate rattle. Replacement demand is rising as these engines age into the 80k–120k mile bracket. Engine code(s): CHZB, CHZJ, DKLA, DKLC, DLLA Compatible models (UK): Fabia (2015–present), Rapid (2015–2019), Scala (2019–present), Kamiq (2019–present), Octavia (2016–present) Production years: 2015–present Common failure points: Wet belt degradation (early 1.0 MPI), injector failure, turbo wastegate rattle
items.cta: Compare Skoda 1.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Skoda 1.2L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.2L HTP (8V/12V) and later 1.2 TSI (EA111/EA211) spanned two decades across Fabia, Roomster, Octavia, Yeti, and Rapid. Early EA111 TSI engines (CBZA, CBZB) are notorious for timing chain tensioner failure, while the HTP is reliable but underpowered. Replacement demand remains strong for 2010–2014 Fabia and Yeti models where chain failure has bent valves. Engine code(s): AWY, BBM, CGP (HTP); CBZA, CBZB, CJZB (1.2 TSI) Compatible models (UK): Fabia (2000–2014), Roomster (2006–2015), Octavia (2010–2014), Yeti (2010–2015), Rapid (2012–2015) Production years: 2000–2015 Common failure points: EA111 TSI – timing chain tensioner failure, injector failure; HTP – valve seat wear
items.cta: Compare Skoda 1.2L petrol engine prices

items.title: Skoda 1.4L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.4L TSI (EA111 and EA211 families) appeared in Octavia, Yeti, Superb, Fabia vRS, and Rapid between 2005 and 2018. Codes include CAXA, CAXC, CZCA, CZDA, and CHYB. The EA111 1.4 TSI is infamous for timing chain stretch and piston ring carbon clogging causing high oil consumption. EA211 units are more reliable but still suffer intake valve carbon buildup on direct‑injection versions. Replacement is common on 2008–2013 Octavia and Yeti. Engine code(s): CAXA, CAXC, CZCA, CZDA, CHYB, BUD Compatible models (UK): Octavia (2005–2018), Yeti (2010–2017), Superb (2008–2015), Fabia vRS (2010–2014), Rapid (2012–2015) Production years: 2005–2018 Common failure points: EA111 – timing chain stretch, ring carbon oil consumption; EA211 – intake valve carbon buildup
items.cta: Compare Skoda 1.4L petrol engine prices

items.title: Skoda 1.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.5L TSI Evo (EA211 evo family) launched in 2017 as a replacement for the 1.4 TSI, now found in Octavia, Superb, Karoq, Kamiq, Scala, and Fabia. Engine codes include DADA, DACA, DPBA, and DZTA. While more efficient than its predecessor, known failure points include kangarooing (cold start hesitation, mostly fixed by software), injector failure, and high‑mileage turbo wastegate issues. Replacement demand is still moderate but growing as fleet cars exceed 80k miles. Engine code(s): DADA, DACA, DPBA, DZTA Compatible models (UK): Octavia (2017–present), Superb (2017–present), Karoq (2017–present), Kamiq (2019–present), Scala (2019–present), Fabia (2021–present) Production years: 2017–present Common failure points: Cold start kangarooing (software fixable), injector failure, turbo wastegate rattle
items.cta: Compare Skoda 1.5L petrol engine prices

items.title: Skoda 1.6L Petrol Engines
items.description: The naturally aspirated 1.6L MPI (EA211 family) is a simple, robust unit fitted to Octavia and Superb primarily for LPG‑friendly fleet markets. Engine codes include CWVA and CWVB. This engine is low‑stress and avoids many TSI problems, but high‑mileage units do require ignition coil packs, camshaft position sensors, and occasionally valve guide wear. Replacement demand is low but steady. Engine code(s): CWVA, CWVB Compatible models (UK): Octavia (2012–2017), Superb (2015–2019) Production years: 2012–2019 Common failure points: Coil pack failure, camshaft position sensor failure, valve guide wear (150k+ miles)
items.cta: Compare Skoda 1.6L petrol engine prices

items.title: Skoda 1.8L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.8L TSI (EA888 Gen1 and Gen2) appeared in Octavia, Superb, and Yeti between 2008 and 2015. Engine codes include CDAA, CJSA, BZB, and CZPA. Known weaknesses include timing chain tensioner failure (pre‑2012), intake valve carbon buildup, and water pump failure. Replacement demand is moderate, mostly from 2008–2012 Octavia and Superb examples where chain failure has caused valve damage. Engine code(s): CDAA, CJSA, BZB, CZPA Compatible models (UK): Octavia (2008–2015), Superb (2008–2015), Yeti (2009–2015) Production years: 2008–2015 Common failure points: Timing chain tensioner failure (pre‑2012), intake valve carbon buildup, water pump leak
items.cta: Compare Skoda 1.8L petrol engine prices

items.title: Skoda 2.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.0L TSI/TFSI (EA888 Gen1 through Gen4) powers performance Skoda models including Octavia vRS, Superb 2.0 TSI, Kodiaq vRS, and high‑spec Karoq. Engine codes include CCZA, CHHA, CJXC, CZPA, DNUA, and DNUE. Common failures vary by generation: Gen1 timing chain tensioner failure, Gen2 intake carbon buildup and failed intake manifolds, Gen3 water pump/thermostat housing leaks, Gen4 (current) minor oil consumption. The vRS badge makes this a high‑value replacement category. Engine code(s): CCZA, CHHA, CJXC, CZPA, DNUA, DNUE, CULA Compatible models (UK): Octavia vRS (2008–present), Superb 2.0 TSI (2015–present), Kodiaq vRS (2018–present), Karoq 2.0 TSI (2018–present) Production years: 2008–present Common failure points: Gen1 – timing chain tensioner; Gen2 – carbon buildup, intake manifold flap failure; Gen3 – water pump housing leak
items.cta: Compare Skoda 2.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Skoda Electric Motors
items.description: Skoda's first all‑electric model, the Enyaq iV, launched in 2020 using the Volkswagen Group MEB platform. It features a rear‑mounted permanent magnet synchronous motor in various power outputs (146bhp to 295bhp across 60/80/80x/vRS variants). Motor codes include EBP (132kW) and EBN (150kW). Electric motor failure is rare, with most driveability issues tracing back to battery management or inverter faults. Replacement is specialist‑only and typically not cost‑effective outside warranty. Motor code(s): EBP (132kW), EBN (150kW), EEV (220kW vRS) Compatible models (UK): Enyaq iV (2020–present) Production years: 2020–present Important notes: Motor failure rare – most issues are battery module or inverter faults. Replacement requires high‑voltage training and specialist diagnostic tools. Closing line: Not sure which engine size your Skoda needs? Enter your registration number and we'll match you to the exact engine code and displacement — instantly. All engines supplied with a minimum 12‑month warranty from UK Skoda specialists. items.title: Skoda Diesel Engines
items.description: Short descriptor: Skoda’s diesel range spans 1.2L to 2.0L TDI units across EA188, EA189, and EA288 families – dominating replacement enquiries (approx. 65% of Skoda engine searches) due to DPF/EGR clogging on short‑journey fleet cars. Common Skoda Diesel Engine Families: EA189 (1.6 TDI – CAYC, CLCA) – Octavia and Yeti favourite; injector loom failure and EGR cooler fires common EA189 (2.0 TDI – BKD, BMN, CFHC) – Octavia vRS diesel and Superb; DPF cracking and turbo vane sticking EA288 (1.6 TDI – CRKB, DBKA, CRVA) – Karoq, Kodiaq, later Octavia; AdBlue injector crystallisation EA288 (2.0 TDI – CKFC, CRLB, DFGA) – Most common Superb/Kodiaq diesel; oil pump belt failure on evo versions EA188 (1.4 TDI – AMF, BNV) – Fabia and Roomster; injector seal leaks, turbo actuator failure Found In: Fabia (2005–2021) – 1.4 TDI (AMF/BNV), 1.6 TDI (CAYC) Rapid (2012–2019) – 1.6 TDI (CAYC/CLCA) Octavia (2004–2020) – 1.6 TDI (CAYC/CLCA/CRKB), 2.0 TDI (BKD/BMN/CKFC/CRLB) Superb (2008–2024) – 1.6 TDI (CAYC/CLCA/DBKA), 2.0 TDI (CFFB/CKFC/DFGA) Yeti (2009–2017) – 1.6 TDI (CAYC), 2.0 TDI (CFHC/CLCB) Karoq (2017–2024) – 1.6 TDI (CRVA), 2.0 TDI (DFGA/DNUA) Kodiaq (2017–present) – 1.6 TDI (CRVA), 2.0 TDI (DFGA/DNUA/DTRB) Known For (Brand‑wide): "Dieselgate" EA189 emissions software and hardware revisions (2015–2017) DPF clogging on short‑journey cars – forced regen often a temporary fix EGR cooler fires on 1.6 EA189 (CAYC) – recall completed but failures persist Injector wiring loom failure on 2.0 BKD and 1.6 CAYC – loss of power on one cylinder AdBlue injector crystallisation on EA288 evo (2017+ 2.0 TDI) Oil pump belt failure on EA288 evo – sudden loss of oil pressure Typical Diesel Models (UK): Octavia 1.6 TDI (CAYC/CLCA/CRKB) Octavia 2.0 TDI vRS (BMN/CKFC) Superb 2.0 TDI (CFFB/CKFC/DFGA) Yeti 1.6 TDI (CAYC) Kodiaq 2.0 TDI (DFGA/DNUA) Fabia 1.4 TDI (BNV)
items.cta: Get quotes for Skoda Diesel replacement engines

items.title: Skoda Petrol Engines
items.description: Short descriptor: Skoda’s petrol line‑up spans naturally aspirated MPI units and turbocharged TSI/TSFI families (EA111, EA211, EA888) – replacement demand is driven by timing chain failures on EA111 1.2/1.4 TSI and carbon buildup on direct‑injection EA888 2.0 TSI. Common Skoda Petrol Engine Families: EA111 1.2 TSI (CBZA, CBZB) – Fabia, Rapid, Yeti; timing chain tensioner failure (2010–2014) EA111 1.4 TSI (CAXA, CAXC) – Octavia, Yeti, Fabia vRS; chain stretch + oil ring carbon clogging EA211 1.0 TSI (CHZB, DKLA) – Fabia, Scala, Kamiq, Octavia; wet belt degradation (early MPI), turbo wastegate rattle EA211 1.4 TSI (CZCA, CZDA) – Octavia, Yeti, Superb; intake valve carbon buildup, water pump leaks EA211 evo 1.5 TSI (DADA, DACA) – Octavia, Karoq, Kamiq, Scala; cold start kangarooing, injector failure EA888 2.0 TSI (CCZA, CHHA, CJXC, CZPA) – Octavia vRS, Superb, Kodiaq vRS; timing chain tensioner (Gen1), water pump leaks (Gen3) MPI 1.0 / 1.6 (CGPA, CWVA) – Fabia, Octavia; simple port injection, coil pack and sensor failures Found In: Fabia (2000–2021) – 1.2 HTP, 1.2 TSI (CBZA/CBZB), 1.4 TSI (CAXA), 1.0 TSI (CHZB), 1.6 MPI (CWVA) Rapid (2012–2019) – 1.2 TSI (CBZA), 1.4 TSI (CZCA), 1.0 TSI (CHZB) Scala (2019–present) – 1.0 TSI (CHZB/DKLA), 1.5 TSI (DADA/DACA) Kamiq (2019–present) – 1.0 TSI (CHZB/DKLA), 1.5 TSI (DADA) Octavia (2005–present) – 1.2 TSI (CBZA), 1.4 TSI (CAXA/CZCA), 1.5 TSI (DADA), 1.8 TSI (CDAA), 2.0 TSI (CCZA/CHHA) Superb (2008–2024) – 1.4 TSI (CZCA), 1.5 TSI (DACA), 1.8 TSI (CDAA), 2.0 TSI (CCZA/CHHA) Yeti (2009–2017) – 1.2 TSI (CBZB), 1.4 TSI (CAXA/CZCA), 1.8 TSI (CDAA) Karoq (2017–present) – 1.0 TSI (DKLA), 1.5 TSI (DACA), 2.0 TSI (CHHA) Kodiaq (2017–present) – 1.5 TSI (DACA), 2.0 TSI (CHHA/CZPA/CULA) Known For (Brand‑wide): EA111 timing chain tensioner failure – catastrophic if ignored (valve/piston contact) Direct‑injection carbon buildup on intake valves – walnut blasting required every 50k miles EA888 Gen2 intake manifold flap failure – causes rough idle and EML EA211 evo "kangarooing" – cold start hesitation, mostly resolved with software High oil consumption on early 1.4 TSI – ring carbon clogging, revised pistons in later units Water pump/thermostat housing leaks across EA211 and EA888 Typical Petrol Models (UK): Octavia vRS 2.0 TSI (CCZA/CHHA) Fabia 1.2 TSI (CBZA) Yeti 1.2 TSI (CBZB) Superb 1.4 TSI (CZCA) Scala 1.0 TSI (CHZB) Kodiaq vRS 2.0 TSI (CULA)
items.cta: Compare Skoda Petrol engine prices

items.title: Skoda Plug‑in Hybrid (PHEV) Engines
items.description: Short descriptor: Skoda’s plug‑in hybrid range uses the VW Group’s PHEV architecture – a 1.4 TSI or 1.5 TSI petrol engine combined with an electric motor and 13kWh battery, offering up to 40 miles of electric‑only range. Common Skoda PHEV Engine Families: EA211 1.4 TSI PHEV (CZEA, DQEA) – Superb iV, Octavia iV; combined 218bhp EA211 evo 1.5 TSI PHEV (DPBA) – Later Superb iV and Kodiaq iV; revised thermal management How Skoda Plug‑in Hybrid Systems Work (brand‑wide): Petrol engine + electric motor drive front wheels through a six‑speed DSG Electric‑only range of 35–40 miles (WLTP) on 13kWh battery (NMC chemistry) Battery sits under rear seats – no cabin space loss Engine replacement rare – most failures are battery modules or hybrid control units The petrol engine block is shared with non‑hybrid EA211 – replacement uses the same core Found In: Superb iV (2019–present) – 1.4 TSI PHEV (CZEA/DQEA), later 1.5 TSI PHEV (DPBA) Octavia iV (2020–present) – 1.4 TSI PHEV (DQEA) Kodiaq iV (2024–present) – 1.5 TSI PHEV (DPBA) Important Notes (Brand‑wide): Plug‑in hybrid versions share the same base EA211 engine block as non‑hybrid 1.4/1.5 TSI Engine replacement requires high‑voltage system disconnection – specialist labour only Hybrid battery health inspection should accompany any engine quote PHEV engine failure is rare – most issues are coolant leaks from the exhaust heat recovery system DSG mechatronics fail more often than the combustion engine on high‑mileage iVs
items.cta: Enquire about Skoda PHEV engine availability

items.title: Skoda Electric Powertrains
items.description: Short descriptor: Skoda’s first dedicated electric model (Enyaq iV) launched in 2020 on the VW Group MEB platform – electric motor failure is extremely rare, but we connect you with UK high‑voltage specialists for motor, inverter, and battery diagnostics. Common Skoda Electric Motors: EBP (132kW / 177bhp) – Enyaq iV 60 (rear motor) EBN (150kW / 201bhp) – Enyaq iV 80 (rear motor) EEV (220kW / 295bhp) – Enyaq vRS (dual motor, front + rear) How Skoda Electric Powertrains Work: Permanent magnet synchronous motor (PMSM) on rear axle; dual‑motor adds front asynchronous unit Battery capacity: 58kWh (60) or 77kWh (80) – usable range up to 340 miles DC fast charging up to 135kW (10–80% in 36 minutes) Motor failure extremely rare – most driveability issues trace to battery modules or inverter faults No traditional "engine" to replace – repairs focus on HV components Found In: Enyaq iV (2020–present) – 60 (EBP rear only), 80 (EBN rear only), vRS (EEV dual motor) Enyaq Coupe iV (2022–present) – same motor options Important Notes: No internal combustion engine – we connect you with HV battery and motor repair specialists Labour rates differ from combustion engine work (lower parts, higher diagnostic time) Motor bearing whine reported on some early 2020–2021 units – replacement under warranty Always request a full HV system health report before buying a used Enyaq with drivetrain noise Battery module replacement is more common than motor replacement (typically £5k–£9k)
items.cta: Enquire about Skoda Electric motor repair or replacement

engineCodeDirectory.h2: Skoda Engine Codes – Most Replaced Engines (2008–2025)
engineCodeDirectory.intro: Every major Skoda engine code from the last two decades, grouped by family, with compatible models and typical reasons why owners seek replacement. Select your engine to compare rebuilt prices and get quotes from UK Skoda specialists.

entries.title: CHZB 1.0 TSI Petrol
entries.description: The CHZB three-cylinder 1.0 TSI powers the Fabia (NJ, 2015–2021), Kamiq (2020–present), Scala (2020–present), and Octavia (5E FL, 2017–2020). Timing chain stretch and carbon buildup on intake valves are the main replacement drivers, particularly on cars used for short journeys. Our rebuilt CHZB units include a revised timing chain kit, fully decarbonised cylinder head, and new injector seals.
entries.cta: Get quotes for CHZB Skoda engine replacement

entries.title: DTSB 1.0 TSI Petrol
entries.description: The DTSB replaced earlier 1.0 TSI variants in the Fabia (NJ, 2018–2021), Scala, and Kamiq. Very similar to CHZB but with updated ECU mapping and a slightly revised valvetrain. Same failure points apply: chain stretch (less common than CHZB but still reported) and carbon buildup. Rebuilt DTSB engines include a fresh timing set, walnut-blasted valves, and new PCV system.

entries.title: CBZA 1.2 TSI Petrol
entries.description: The CBZA 1.2 TSI was fitted to the Fabia (NJ, 2010–2014), Rapid (NH, 2012–2019), Octavia (5E, 2012–2017), and Yeti (5L, 2009–2015). Timing chain tensioner failure (pre‑2014) is the #1 catastrophic failure — chain jump bends valves. High oil consumption from stuck piston rings is the #2 replacement driver. Our rebuilt CBZA units include an upgraded chain tensioner kit, new piston rings, and a fully serviced oil pump.

entries.title: CHYB 1.2 TSI Petrol
entries.description: Successor to CBZA in later Fabia (NJ, 2014–2018) and Rapid models. A revised tensioner reduced sudden failure rates, but chain stretch still occurs by 60,000–90,000 miles. Oil consumption remains an issue. Rebuilt CHYB units include a new timing chain set, revised oil separator, and decarbonised intake valves.
entries.cta: Get quotes for CHYB 1.2 TSI engine

entries.title: CRMB 1.6 MPI Petrol
entries.description: The CRMB is a naturally aspirated 1.6 MPI (multi-port injection) — no turbo, no direct injection. Fitted to Fabia (NJ), Octavia (5E), and Rapid. Extremely reliable, but high-mileage units (>120,000 miles) suffer piston ring wear, oil consumption, and occasional head gasket failure. Rebuilt CRMB engines include new piston rings, valve stem seals, timing chain, and water pump.

entries.title: CLCA 1.6 TDI Diesel
entries.description: The CLCA 1.6 TDI powers a huge range: Octavia (5E, 2012–2020), Fabia (NJ, 2014–2021), Rapid (NH), Yeti (5L, 2014–2017), and Karoq (NU). DPF regeneration failure (short trips), EGR cooler leaks, and intake manifold flap failure are the top three replacement triggers. Our rebuilt CLCA units include a cleaned or new DPF, replacement EGR valve/cooler, and a new timing belt kit.
entries.cta: Get quotes for CLCA 1.6 TDI engine replacement

entries.title: DBKA 1.6 TDI Diesel
entries.description: The DBKA is a later 1.6 TDI variant (Euro 6) fitted to Octavia (5E FL, 2016–2020), Fabia (NJ, 2017–2021), Karoq (NU), and Kodiaq (NS). Same EA288 architecture as CLCA — DPF/EGR failures dominate. Additionally, the AdBlue system (SCR) can fail, though this usually doesn't require a full engine replacement. Rebuilt DBKA engines include DPF clean/replacement, new EGR valve, and injector testing.

entries.title: CFHA 2.0 TDI 170 Diesel
entries.description: The CFHA 2.0 TDI (170hp) appeared in the Octavia vRS diesel (5E, 2013–2017), Superb (3T/3V), and Yeti (5L). High-pressure fuel pump (HPFP) failure sending metal debris through the injection system is a known catastrophic failure. DPF and EGR issues also occur. Our rebuilt CFHA units include a new HPFP, fully flushed fuel rail and injectors, DPF clean, and new timing belt.

entries.title: CRVC 2.0 TDI 150 Diesel
entries.description: The most common 2.0 TDI in the range — CRVC (150hp) powers the Octavia (5E), Superb (3V), Kodiaq (NS), Karoq (NU), and Yeti (5L). DPF/EGR failures, turbocharger sticking vanes, and intake manifold flap failure drive most replacements. Rebuilt CRVC units include a professionally cleaned DPF, new EGR cooler, turbo service or replacement, and new timing belt.
entries.cta: Get quotes for CRVC 2.0 TDI engine

entries.title: CUNA 2.0 TDI 150 Diesel
entries.description: The CUNA is a later EA288 Evo variant for the Superb (3V, 2018–present) and Kodiaq (NS, 2018–present). Dual AdBlue injection and a more complex EGR system. Failures are less common, but DPF clogging (short trips) and EGR cooler leaks still occur. Rebuilt CUNA engines include DPF clean, new EGR system components, and updated software calibration where applicable.
entries.cta: Compare CUNA Skoda diesel engine prices

entries.title: CWZA 2.0 TDI 190 Diesel
entries.description: The top-output 2.0 TDI (190hp, twin‑dosed AdBlue) fitted to the Kodiaq (NS, 2017–present) and Superb (3V, 2018–present). Sticking turbo vanes (overboost/underboost codes) and DPF regeneration issues are the main replacement drivers. Rebuilt CWZA units include a reconditioned or new turbo, DPF clean, new EGR cooler, and revised oil separator.
entries.cta: Get quotes for CWZA 2.0 TDI 190 engine

entries.title: CCZA 1.8 TFSI Petrol
entries.description: The CCZA 1.8 TFSI (EA888 Gen2) was fitted to the Octavia (1Z, 2008–2013), Superb (3T, 2008–2015), and Yeti (5L, 2009–2015). Timing chain tensioner failure (pre‑2012) is catastrophic — chain jump bends valves. Carbon buildup on intake valves causes misfires. Our rebuilt CCZA units include the revised Gen3 timing chain tensioner, a full decarbonisation (walnut blasting), and a new water pump.

entries.title: CRGC 1.8 TSI Petrol
entries.description: The CRGC (EA888 Gen3, 180hp) appeared in the Octavia (5E, 2013–2017) and Superb (3V, 2015–2018). Improved timing chain tensioner but still suffers carbon buildup and water pump leaks. Oil pump drive degradation is a known issue on higher-mileage units. Rebuilt CRGC engines include a decarbonised head, new water pump, upgraded oil pump drive, and new timing set.
entries.cta: Get quotes for CRGC 1.8 TSI engine replacement

entries.title: CHHA 2.0 TSI 190 Petrol
entries.description: The CHHA 2.0 TSI (EA888 Gen3, 190hp) powers the Octavia (5E), Superb (3V), Kodiaq (NS), and Karoq (NU). Carbon buildup on intake valves is the #1 long-term issue. Water pump leaks and thermostat housing cracks are also common. Rebuilt CHHA units include walnut-blasted valves, new water pump/thermostat housing, revised PCV, and new timing chain set.

entries.title: CRTD 2.0 TFSI Petrol
entries.description: The CRTD is the performance variant (220–230hp) found in the Octavia vRS (5E, 2013–2020) and Superb (3V, 2015–2020). Same carbon buildup issues as CHHA, plus increased risk of timing chain stretch on tuned examples. Rebuilt CRTD units include full decarbonisation, upgraded timing chain kit, new water pump, and performance-oriented head gasket (where applicable).
entries.cta: Get quotes for CRTD 2.0 TFSI engine replacement

entries.title: DNUB 2.0 TSI 190 Petrol
entries.description: The DNUB is a later EA888 Gen3B Miller-cycle engine (190hp) with improved thermal efficiency. Fitted to the Octavia (5E FL, 2017–2020), Kodiaq (NS), and Karoq (NU). Carbon buildup still occurs but takes longer to cause symptoms. Water pump leaks remain common. Rebuilt DNUB units include decarbonised valves, new water pump assembly, revised PCV, and new timing chain kit.

entries.title: BNM 1.4 TDI Diesel
entries.description: The BNM three-cylinder 1.4 TDI (75–80hp) powered the Fabia (5J, 2005–2014), Roomster (2006–2015), and Rapid (NH, 2012–2014). Injector loom failure inside the cylinder head causes misfires and limp mode. EGR valve clogging and turbo actuator failure are also common. Rebuilt BNM units include a new injector loom, cleaned EGR valve, and reconditioned turbo actuator.
entries.cta: Get quotes for BNM 1.4 TDI engine

entries.title: BLS 1.9 TDI Diesel
entries.description: The BLS 1.9 TDI PD (Pumpe Düse) was fitted to the Octavia (1Z, 2004–2010), Fabia (5J, 2007–2010), and Roomster. Camshaft lobe wear (where the cam actuates the unit injectors) and in-head injector wiring loom failure are the two dominant replacement drivers. Rebuilt BLS units include a new camshaft, new injector loom, and new timing belt kit.

entries.title: AZQ 1.2 HTP Petrol
entries.description: The AZQ 1.2 HTP (6-valve) was fitted to the Fabia (5J, 2003–2010) and Roomster (2006–2010). A simple 3-cylinder, chain-driven unit. Valve seat recession (running on LPG or poor maintenance) and timing chain stretch are the main issues. Rebuilt AZQ units include a new timing chain kit, re-cut valve seats, and new valve stem seals. Closing line (Block A): Can't find your engine code listed above? engineYears.h2: Skoda Engines by Year – What Was Fitted & When
engineYears.intro: This section covers Skoda's complete UK engine timeline from 1998 to the present across every major model line - from the pre-VW Group 1.9 SDI/TDI era through the landmark EA111/EA113 petrol families, the high-demand 1.9 TDI and 2.0 TDI diesels, the EA888 TSI turbo petrol generation, the EA211/EA288 modular era, and the current iV PHEV and Enyaq electric range - so owners can identify exactly what was fitted to their vehicle by year and compare rebuilt Skoda engine prices from UK specialists.

years.preview: VW Group Integration Bedding In: 1.9 TDI & 1.6/2.0 MPI Era
years.description: Short intro: The post-VW Group integration Skoda range was built around Volkswagen's proven EA827/EA111 petrol and the 1.9 SDI/TDI diesel families - giving Skoda buyers access to VW Group reliability for the first time across the Octavia, Fabia, and Felicia. Key Changes First-generation Octavia (1996 launch, UK volume from 1998) - 1.6 MPI, 1.8 20v, 2.0 MPI, 1.9 SDI, 1.9 TDI Fabia first generation launched (1999) - 1.4 MPI, 1.0 MPI, 1.9 SDI, 1.9 TDI (100 hp) 1.9 TDI PD (Pumpe Düse unit injector) introduced across the VW Group range - significant power increase over pre-PD TDI Felicia continues until 2001 with 1.3 MPI and 1.9 D/SDI - final non-VW engine Skoda Euro 2/3 compliance in force Main Engines AGR / AHF / ASV - 1.9 TDI (90 hp) (Octavia, Fabia diesel) ALH - 1.9 TDI (110 hp) (Octavia vRS TDI) AEE / AKK - 1.6 MPI (Octavia, Fabia) AHL / AEH - 1.6 petrol (Octavia) AQY / APK - 2.0 MPI petrol (Octavia) AGP / AYQ - 1.9 SDI (Fabia, Octavia) ARX / AUM - 1.8 20v turbo (Octavia 1.8T) Popular Models Skoda Octavia (first generation) Skoda Fabia (first generation) Skoda Felicia Known For 1.9 TDI (pre-PD) is one of the most reliable diesel engines ever produced - robust pump-and-injector design, exceptional longevity; UK specialists regularly see examples exceeding 250,000 miles with correct timing belt servicing Timing belt failure on 1.9 TDI is catastrophic and completely preventable - belt service every 60,000 miles (not the stated 90,000) is the UK specialist consensus for peace of mind; this single maintenance item generates the highest volume of Skoda engine replacement enquiries for this era 1.8 20v turbo Octavia is a capable, enjoyable engine - early units suffer oil sludge from extended service intervals but long-lived when properly maintained 1.9 SDI naturally aspirated diesel is notably underpowered but virtually indestructible - preferred choice for ultra-high-mileage fleet operators due to its simplicity [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: AGR, AHF, ASV, ALH, AEE, AKK, AHL, AEH, AQY, APK, AGP, AYQ, ARX, AUM, AEW, AZE
years.cta: Find rebuilt 1998–2001 Skoda 1.9 TDI and 1.8T engine prices from UK specialists

years.preview: Superb First Generation, 1.9 TDI PD Peak & 1.4 16v Introduction
years.description: Short intro: The first-generation Superb extended Skoda upmarket with VW Group B-segment platform engines, the Octavia RS debuted the 1.8T in performance trim, and the 1.9 TDI PD (Pumpe Düse) unit-injector diesel became the most widely fitted Skoda diesel in UK history. Key Changes Superb first generation (2002) - first Skoda executive saloon; 1.8T, 2.5 TDI V6 (BDG), and 2.8 V6 petrol 1.9 TDI PD (BKC, BXE, BLS) replaces pre-PD units across Octavia and Fabia - 100/105 hp Octavia RS launched with 1.8T 180 hp - benchmark Skoda performance model Fabia continues with 1.4 16v (BBY, BKY) petrol and 1.9 TDI (AMF 75 hp) diesel Room for Yeti previewed on Octavia platform Euro 3 dominant; Euro 4 preparation begins Main Engines BKC / BXE / BLS - 1.9 TDI PD 100–105 hp (Octavia, Fabia, Superb) AMF - 1.9 TDI PD 100 hp (Fabia 100) AWT / ARX - 1.8 20v turbo 150/180 hp (Octavia RS, Superb 1.8T) BDG - 2.5 TDI V6 (Superb 2.5 TDI - VW Group unit) AZX - 2.8 V6 petrol (Superb 2.8) BBY / BKY - 1.4 16v petrol (Fabia) BFQ / BFJ - 1.6 MPI petrol (Octavia, Fabia) Popular Models Skoda Octavia (first generation) Skoda Fabia (first generation) Skoda Superb (first generation) Skoda Octavia RS (first generation) Known For 1.9 TDI PD units (BKC, BXE, BLS) are the UK's most commercially significant Skoda diesel replacement engines - extremely widespread, excellent long-term durability, but timing belt replacement at 60,000-mile intervals remains absolutely critical; this engine generates peak UK Skoda replacement search volume across this period 1.9 TDI PD is susceptible to EGR valve carbon fouling in urban use - EGR blanking or regular chemical cleaning is a standard UK independent specialist recommendation for high-mileage examples 2.5 TDI V6 Superb is a smooth and capable unit but cam belt failures and oil leaks from front seals are the most cited concerns - considered higher-maintenance than the 1.9 TDI 1.8T 180 hp Octavia RS is a well-regarded performance engine - coil pack failure and oil consumption from high-mileage PCV system wear are the most common UK specialist diagnoses [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: BKC, BXE, BLS, AMF, AWT, ARX, BDG, AZX, BBY, BKY, BFQ, BFJ, AGR (continued), AHF (continued)
years.cta: Compare 2002–2004 Skoda Octavia, Fabia, and Superb engine prices from UK specialists

years.preview: Octavia Second Generation, 2.0 TDI & EA113 2.0 TSI Introduction 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Short intro: The second-generation Octavia (2004 launch, UK volume from 2005) brought VW Group's new EA113 2.0 TSI turbocharged petrol and the EA188 2.0 TDI CR common-rail diesel - an engineering step change that defined the Skoda range for the following decade. Key Changes Octavia second generation (2004 global, 2005 UK dominant) - 1.6 MPI, 1.8 TSI, 2.0 TDI CR, 1.9 TDI PD 2.0 TDI CR (AZV, BKD) introduced - common-rail replaces PD unit injector in Octavia and Superb; 140 hp 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction 2.0 TSI EA113 (AXX, BWA, BPY) introduced in Octavia vRS (200 hp) - turbocharged direct-injection four-cylinder 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction Roomster launched (2006) with 1.4 TDI (BMS, BNV) and 1.6 MPI (BSF) Superb second generation previewed Euro 4 enforced from 2006 Main Engines AZV / BKD - 2.0 TDI CR 140 hp (Octavia, Superb) BXE / BKC - 1.9 TDI PD 105 hp (Octavia, Fabia continued) AXX / BWA / BPY - 2.0 TSI EA113 200 hp (Octavia vRS) BSE / BSF - 1.6 MPI (Octavia, Roomster) BMD - 1.4 TSI (Octavia, Fabia preview) BMS / BNV - 1.4 TDI PD (Roomster, Fabia diesel) Popular Models Skoda Octavia (second generation) Skoda Fabia (second generation) Skoda Roomster Skoda Octavia vRS Skoda Superb (first generation final) Known For 2.0 TDI CR (BKD, AZV) is a significant reliability improvement over PD units for some failure modes - but timing belt still required at 60,000–75,000 miles; EGR valve and DPF fouling in urban use are the primary UK specialist complaints 2.0 TSI EA113 (AXX, BWA) in Octavia vRS suffers from the notorious early timing chain tensioner fault - plastic collar on the original tensioner can fail, allowing chain to jump and destroy the engine; this is one of the most significant VW Group petrol engine reliability incidents of its era 1.9 TDI PD continues to generate strong UK replacement demand as the large pre-2005 fleet ages - 2005–2007 period is the peak year for 1.9 TDI engine failure and replacement across the entire Skoda range 1.4 TDI PD in Roomster and Fabia is a compact, fuel-efficient unit but timing belt failure and injector wear at higher mileages are well-documented [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: AZV, BKD, BXE, BKC, AXX, BWA, BPY, BSE, BSF, BMD, BMS, BNV, AMF (continued)
years.cta: Get 2005–2007 Skoda Octavia, vRS, and Fabia diesel engine replacement quotes from UK specialists

years.preview: Superb Second Gen, Yeti, 1.4 TSI Twincharged & EA888 Arrives 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Short intro: The second-generation Superb and the Yeti expanded the Skoda range significantly, the 1.4 TSI twincharged engine introduced Skoda's most technically innovative petrol unit, and the EA888 1.8/2.0 TSI family began replacing EA113 as VW Group's volume turbocharged petrol. Key Changes Superb second generation (2008) - 1.8 TSI (EA888), 2.0 TDI CR, 3.6 V6 FSI - Skoda's largest and most sophisticated model Yeti launched (2009) - first Skoda SUV; 1.2 TSI, 1.8 TSI, 2.0 TDI CR 1.4 TSI twincharged (CAXA, BMY) - supercharger + turbocharger; 125/140 hp; Fabia vRS and Octavia 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction EA888 1.8 TSI (BZB, CDAA, CABA) introduced - replaces EA113 1.8T across volume models 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction EA189 2.0 TDI CR updated (CBAB, CFFB) - replaces BKD/AZV in Octavia, Superb, Yeti Euro 5 preparation begins Main Engines CDAA / CABA / BZB - 1.8 TSI EA888 Gen 1 (Octavia, Superb) CAXA / BMY - 1.4 TSI twincharged 125/140 hp (Fabia vRS, Octavia 1.4) CBAB / CFFB - 2.0 TDI CR EA189 140/170 hp (Octavia, Superb, Yeti) CFGB / CLCA - 2.0 TDI 140/170 hp (Superb, Yeti) AXX continued / CDLF - 2.0 TSI EA113/EA888 200 hp (Octavia vRS) CHFA / CGPA - 1.2 TSI (Yeti, Fabia - EA111) Popular Models Skoda Superb (second generation) Skoda Yeti Skoda Octavia (second generation) Skoda Fabia vRS Skoda Octavia vRS Known For EA888 Gen 1 1.8 TSI timing chain is significantly more reliable than EA113 - however early Gen 1 units (2008–2011) still show chain stretch and tensioner wear from 80,000–100,000 miles; cold-start chain rattle is the first warning sign 1.4 TSI twincharged is a technical marvel - supercharger for instant low-end response, turbo takes over higher in the rev range; however supercharger drive belt and clutch wear from 60,000 miles makes this a higher-maintenance petrol option; timing belt failure remains catastrophic EA189 2.0 TDI CR introduces Dieselgate-era emissions software - while not directly an engine failure, 2008–2015 EA189 diesels are the focus of the NOx defeat device scandal (VW Group, 2015); no mechanical impact on engine reliability but EGR and DPF modifications from the fix can affect performance Yeti 2.0 TDI is a very capable and practical car - 4x4 versions with CBAB/CFFB units considered among the most trouble-free UK diesel crossovers of this era [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: CDAA, CABA, BZB, CAXA, BMY, CBAB, CFFB, CFGB, CLCA, CDLF, CHFA, CGPA, CBZA, CJZA
years.cta: Source 2008–2010 Skoda Superb, Yeti, and Octavia engine quotes from UK specialists

years.preview: Octavia Third Generation Preview, Dieselgate EA189 Era & Euro 5 Peak 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Short intro: The EA111 1.2 TSI became Skoda's most widely fitted entry petrol engine, the EA188/EA189 2.0 TDI range reached peak UK market penetration across Octavia, Superb, and Yeti, and the Octavia third generation was previewed with full EA211/EA288 engine preparation. Key Changes EA111 1.2 TSI (CBZB, CBZA, CJZA) dominant in Fabia, Yeti, and Roomster - 86/105 hp turbocharged three/four-cylinder 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction Rapid launched (2012) - new model; 1.2 TSI and 1.6 TDI CR Octavia third generation revealed (2012) - full launch 2013; EGR valve and DPF maintenance the primary ongoing cost; Dieselgate emissions fix (applied from 2016) can sometimes lead to increased EGR deposits and DPF frequency issues 1.2 TSI EA111 is frugal and practical but prone to timing chain wear on early CBZB units - chain stretch and cam cover oil leaks from 60,000 miles; faq.h2: Skoda Engine Replacement – Frequently Asked Questions
faq.intro: Quick, honest answers to the most common questions Skoda owners ask before comparing engine replacement prices and finding a UK specialist.

items.question: How much does a Skoda engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A replacement Skoda engine typically costs between £1,200 and £2,800 for common units like the 1.9 TDI (BXE/BLS) or 1.2 TSI (CBZA), rising to £3,000–£5,500 for the 2.0 TDI (CKFC/CRLB) or 2.0 TSI (CHHA) used in Octavia vRS and Kodiaq. Reconditioned engines usually run £1,800–£3,500 fitted, while supply‑only can knock £400–£800 off the total. The final price varies significantly by engine code and condition — compare multiple quotes to ensure you're not overpaying. → Compare Skoda engine replacement prices now
items.cta: Compare Skoda engine prices now

items.question: What affects the cost of a Skoda engine replacement?
items.answer: Four main factors drive the price: engine size and family (1.2 TSI EA111 costs far less than 2.0 TSI EA888), condition (used vs rebuilt vs remanufactured), vehicle age and complexity (Kodiaq 4x4 takes more labour than Fabia FWD), and whether you choose supply‑only or full supply‑and‑fit. London and South East garages typically charge 20–30% more than Northern or Midlands specialists for the same job. Our comparison tool brings transparency to these variables — you'll see exactly what each supplier includes. → Get quotes from UK Skoda engine specialists
items.cta: Compare Skoda engine prices now

items.question: Is it worth replacing the engine in my Skoda?
items.answer: In most cases, yes — especially if the chassis, gearbox, and bodywork are solid. A professionally rebuilt Skoda engine with upgraded components (e.g., revised timing chain tensioner on EA111 1.2/1.4 TSI) will often outlast the original and costs far less than replacing the entire vehicle. The alternative is buying a used car of unknown history, which carries its own risks. If your Skoda holds reasonable residual value (e.g., Octavia vRS, Superb Laurin & Klement), engine replacement is almost always the smarter financial decision. → Use our free comparison tool to see whether a rebuilt or used Skoda engine makes financial sense

items.question: What's included in a Skoda engine supply and fit service?
items.answer: A full supply‑and‑fit package typically includes sourcing the correct engine code for your Skoda, removal of the failed unit, installation of the replacement engine, new oil, filters, coolant, a workmanship warranty (usually 12 months), disposal of the old engine, and a road test to confirm proper operation. Fitting costs generally add £400–£800 to the supply price, depending on model complexity. Always confirm what's included — some quotes exclude ancillaries like the turbo or EGR cooler. → Compare Skoda engine supply and fit deals

items.question: How long does a Skoda engine replacement take?
items.answer: For common Skoda models like the Fabia 1.2 TSI or Octavia 2.0 TDI, a like‑for‑like swap typically takes 3–5 working days from order to road‑ready. More complex models (Kodiaq vRS, Superb 4x4) may extend to 5–7 days due to additional drivetrain components. The biggest variable is engine availability — our network of UK specialists stocks frequently‑replaced codes (e.g., CAYC 1.6 TDI, CBZA 1.2 TSI, CKFC 2.0 TDI), but rarer units may add 2–3 days for delivery. Ask each supplier for their current lead time before committing. → Find reconditioned Skoda engines with comprehensive warranty cover

items.question: Do reconditioned Skoda engines come with warranty?
items.answer: Yes — every reconditioned Skoda engine supplied through EngineMarket carries a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty. Many rebuilders also offer extended 24‑month coverage on remanufactured units that include new pistons, rings, and timing components. The warranty typically covers major internal parts: crankshaft, con rods, pistons, cylinder head, timing chain/belt, oil pump, and gaskets. Always confirm exact terms, labour coverage limits, and any exclusions (e.g., injectors, turbo, ancillary components) directly with your chosen supplier before purchase. → Compare Skoda engine replacement prices now
items.cta: Find Skoda engines with warranty

items.question: Should I choose a used or reconditioned Skoda engine?
items.answer: Used engines are cheaper upfront (often £600–£1,200 less) and make sense as a temporary fix or if you plan to sell the car within a year. However, you inherit the original failure risks — including known Skoda issues like EA111 timing chain stretch or EA288 DPF clogging. Reconditioned engines cost more (£1,800–£3,500 fitted) but include new wear components (rings, bearings, seals, timing kit) and a 12‑month warranty, typically delivering another 100k+ miles. On failure‑prone Skoda engines (1.2 TSI, 1.4 TSI, 1.6 TDI), a reconditioned unit with upgraded parts is strongly recommended over a used donor. → Get quotes from UK Skoda engine specialists

items.question: What are the most common Skoda engine failures?
items.answer: The most common catastrophic failures driving Skoda engine replacement include: EA111 1.2/1.4 TSI timing chain tensioner failure (Fabia, Rapid, Yeti, 2010–2014), EA189 1.6 TDI DPF/EGR clogging leading to turbo backpressure failure (Octavia, Yeti, 2010–2015), EA288 2.0 TDI oil pump belt snapping on evo versions (Superb, Kodiaq, 2019–2022), EA888 Gen1/2 2.0 TSI timing chain stretch and intake carbon buildup (Octavia vRS, 2008–2013), and 1.4 TSI piston ring carbon clogging causing high oil consumption (Fabia vRS, Yeti, 2010–2016). Early detection rarely prevents these from becoming terminal — replacement is the most common outcome. → Compare Skoda engine replacement prices for these common failure codes

items.question: How can I get the best price on a Skoda engine?
items.answer: Compare at least three quotes — prices for the same Skoda engine code can vary by £1,000+ between suppliers. Consider used (£800–£1,800) for tight budgets or short‑term ownership, but upgrade to a reconditioned unit (£1,800–£3,500) with a 12‑month warranty if you plan to keep the car. Ask whether the quote is supply‑only or includes fitting (fitters charge £400–£800 extra), whether delivery and VAT are included, and whether the old engine disposal is covered. Avoid the cheapest offer if it excludes warranty or uses unknown‑history donor engines. Transparency saves money. → Use our free comparison tool to find the best price for your Skoda engine
